source: AXIS Foil Riders Facebook Group + AXIS Foils Official Page

posts.author: Nick Restivo
posts.title: Which wings are easy to pump on SUP?
posts.fullText: I'm 175lbs and currently SUP foil, para wing (+dw), and dockstart on the 1300v1. I also have an Art Pro 1201 and a 999v2… but I cannot consistently pump either of them for the life of me. I hear that the 1201 is supposed to be a pump machine, but as soon as I loose speed it'll drop out hard on me. I also can't flat water popup on it, but the 1300 is starting to feel real big in the surf and slow in the bumps. Is there a larger Surge coming that might fill this gap? Does something like a FB 1350 make more sense even though it has the same area as 1201? Or do I just have a gap in technique that not letting me pump my 1201 efficiently on SUP? Thanks for any thoughts here. Also my tail is a 360/45 and I ride the armstrong dw v1 107L if important
posts.discipline: SUP foil, para wing, downwind, dockstart

comments.author: Mark Shinn
comments.text: Hi Nick, Good news and Bad..... the good news.... yes the Fireball 1350 is a very very easy foil to pump. It is amazingly efficient for glide and forward speed. It will air very nicely with your skinny 45. That was the good news. Now the bad.... the 1201 is not the easiest foil to pump but it is a very good pump foil and if you are loosing height on the mast and then dropping out the sky it is unfortunately a technique failure. As a start place In would guess your pump action is to vertical and you are not generating forward motion with the legs..... Will spending money improve things - yes Will spending money fix the under-lying issue - no probably not.

replies.author: Nick Restivo
replies.text: thats honestly kind of double good news. would rather not spend more money, but yea it seems I have training to do… any tips on pumping the 1201 or these lower area wings? Would it help to learn on a lighter board or is it more about speed and mast height than weight? Thanks for the reply

comments.author: Charles Bessard
comments.text: Did you try the png1300v2? Of course one shouldn't use it in overhead conditions but it does handle some size and I find it quite nice in the turn. For me it works for dock start and DW (wing and PW) and light wind wing. It is not as fast as the 1201 but it has really good glide. It is a bigger foil than the 1201 and one feels it but it has a very smooth and not locked in turn for my weight 95kg and my mellow style of riding: bay runs with short period wind chop between knee high to waist high occasional chest high. When it is consistently bigger than waist high I change to the 1201. Or 1180 depending on conditions

replies.author: Nick Restivo
replies.text: I have really wanted to try the v2, just didn't want to shell out for it just yet with my 1201 sitting there as supposedly a better wing.. are you able to pump yours consistently?

comments.author: Jake David
comments.text: The 1201 is a great sup surf foil that pups amazing with the right stab. Your problem is too small a stab. Not sure why but on the sup I can only pump for 2-3 waves with the skinny 45 but with the progressive 375 I can connect 10+ waves. A large skinny or large surf skinny may help too but haven't tried yet. Also make sure your mast is in the right place

replies.author: Jake David
replies.text: technique is your main issue though. Ride high on the mast and make a full extension with your legs. You should be breaching occasionally since your pushing the height limit for max efficiency

comments.author: Angus Mackay
comments.text: The 1201 is a solid wing but doesn't like to go slow so you just need to work on your technique and output level and keep the speed up. You should be able to dockstart and pump it in flat water for 45 seconds so work on getting there. It's a bit of a bitch to start though, get a run. The real question you should be asking though, is how fast are your bumps? It sounds like you have pretty slow bumps in general, and if that's the case you'll have a lot more fun on the 1300v2 or FB 1350. People always tout how small a foil they ride but if your goal is to go out and ride bumps you want to have something that'll get you through the worst patch of them. I ride my 1340 sqcm foil 90% of the time as where I live we just don't get fast bumps.

posts.author: Andrew Clayton
posts.fullText: I kite on surge 740, adv+ ultrashort, p275. It's the most playful setup iv ever tried! Decided to try that setup with my FD assist +. I couldn't flat water start it, needed a little bump. Had great glide and super easy to pump for such a small foil but it was super twitchy especially controlling pitch. Any one tried similar setup, would a bigger stab help? Thanks
posts.setup: Surge 740, ADV+ Ultrashort, P275, Foil Drive Assist+

comments.author: Paul Hurley
comments.text: I had the same problem with my surge 830 adv+ultra 320ss Put a 1 deg positive shim on the tail Settled down the twichness and had much better pitch control.

comments.author: Mark Shinn
comments.text: There is a huge difference between kite foiling and Foil Drive riding. The kite is like a 3rd leg.... get rid of it and you suddenly notice how unstable foiling can be. Not sure your weight but 740 is a small foil to start on a foil drive for most riders, at 100 kg I am starting the 830 but need a decent push from chop or a wave. Back to the main point.... a larger stab will increase the pitch sensitivity but so will time. I would put in a couple more sessions and let your ears adjust... it's not an inherently unstable set up so it is just getting use to it. If you want an instant fix then longer fuse or bigger stab will help but at the cost of some of the roll and turn ability. Shimming the stab can also calm it down but can also make it hard to take off especially on the Foil Drive.

comments.author: Sean Richardson
comments.text: A bigger stab can help. I ride all the Surges with progressive stabs. Was just out on the 780 and 300p… I use the smaller stabs for bigger foils, so 275p on 830 and 890 and 250p on 1010.

nearbyPosts_within24h.author: Mats Nilsson Hahne
nearbyPosts_within24h.fullText: 80 kgs, looking to learn parawing on 90 litre midlength. Surge 1010 or Spitfire 1030/1100? I have the Surge 950, even if it gets up real easy I think it is on the small side for me to learn parawing? Edit: I am a fairly decent wingfoiler (gybes, tacks, jumps) and have been foiling since 2021.
keyInsights: Mark Shinn (AXIS): Surge 1010 is 100% the way to go for parawing - uses it at ~100kg
Sean Richardson: Surge 1010 gets going as easily as SF 1030, glides much better
Stephen Parker: Para size matters more than foil - 4.3 para needs 18 knots min at 80kg
Doug D PFoiler: SF 1100 probably best to learn on
Fraser Shaw: Surge 1010 may be too reactive for learning
Tom Benjamin: 1100 is good learner size for new discipline
Peter Hanning: Uses Surge 1010 for parawing at 96kg on 125L DW board, also FB 1350 for light winds
James Moolenschot: Recommends ART v2 1099 for parawing learning - extra glide helps during gybes
Igor Mayorov: Tried both 1010 and ART v2 1099 - Surge better in turns, ART has better glide
